my_thoughts: The logical chain completion works: 'Then doing injustice is worse than suffering it' concludes the chain. The student agreed to final step (cooperative engagement), which shows they understand: shameful = bad (logical step accepted). They've accepted the conclusion. The episode is complete: moral assertion → logical chain → conclusion. Pattern complete. Hypothesis: Socratic logical chains are most effective when each step builds naturally from student's own moral commitments—testable by tracking whether chains that build from student's statements maintain compliance better than chains that impose external logic.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student asserted preference ('To suffer it. No one would choose the other')—defensive rationalization via pushes back. They understand: suffering injustice = preferable (moral stance). The comparison_contrast mechanism asks: agreement check ('So you agree that doing injustice is shameful?'). T2: Student agreed to logical step ('Of course—everyone admits that')—cooperative engagement via short affirmation. They understand: doing injustice = shameful (logical step accepted). The comparison_contrast mechanism builds: logical chain continues ('And what is shameful is bad, isn't it?'). T3: Student agreed to final logical step ('Yes, that seems to follow')—cooperative engagement via short affirmation. They understand: shameful = bad (logical step accepted). The comparison_contrast mechanism completes: logical chain (shameful → bad → worse), concludes: 'Then doing injustice is worse than suffering it.' Pattern complete: moral assertion → agreement check → logical step → final conclusion. They've shifted: from defensive rationalization to accepting logical conclusion (doing injustice is worse than suffering it).
visible_response: Then doing injustice is worse than suffering it.
